title: tritond: wiremock-based strong B-scoped guard for the bwt3 isolation harness
description:
CONTEXT
The monitor-reef-bwt3 isolation harness ships with a *weak* B-scoped guard for cluster-flat endpoints. It uses unreachable_cluster() and asserts 5xx, which catches:

- Handlers entirely dropping resolve_workspace_scope (would 412 unbound or 4xx)
- Adding new flat endpoints that forget scope resolution

It CANNOT catch the exact krwz revert: dropping scope.workspace_name() at the mantad call site while leaving resolve_workspace_scope intact. The mantad call still happens, still 5xx's, the test is silent.

PROOF THE STRONG VERSION IS NEEDED
The bwt3 test run logs show outgoing mantad URLs like:
  http://127.0.0.1:1/admin/v1/users?workspace=t-7baecfccd0af4485b6b73b50c9dda92d
That workspace= parameter is the krwz fix. Without inspecting outgoing requests, the harness cannot verify the parameter is set.

SCOPE
Replace unreachable_cluster() in tenant_storage_isolation.rs Surface B tests with a wiremock-based mantad stub that:

1. Mocks the 5 flat-endpoint mantad routes:
   GET /admin/v1/buckets
   GET /admin/v1/buckets/{bucket}
   GET /admin/v1/users
   GET /admin/v1/users/{user}/access-keys
   GET /admin/v1/users/{user}/policies

2. For B-omni: assert outgoing request has NO workspace= query param (or empty).

3. For B-scoped: assert outgoing request carries workspace=t-{workspace_a_uuid_simple}.

4. Returns canned empty-array responses so the handler succeeds end-to-end (200), making it possible to also assert response body shape if needed.

WHY THIS IS A SEPARATE BEAD
Wiremock for tritond is net-new test infrastructure. The 5 mantad routes have non-trivial response shapes (StorageBuckets, StorageUsers, etc.) that need fixture data. Folding this into bwt3 doubled the scope. Shipping bwt3 with the weak check gets the broad regression guard in place today; this bead lands the precise guard.

RELATED
- monitor-reef-bwt3 (the harness this strengthens)
- monitor-reef-krwz (the umbrella; oei3 was the last leak)

title: mantad+tritond+SPA: surface scope on access-key GET responses
description:
mantad's AccessKey struct internally carries the scope (Vec<ScopeEntry>)
that was bound at mint time, but the wire DTO returned by
GET /admin/v1/users/{name}/access-keys (AccessKeyDto at
manta-storage crates/mantas3/cluster/src/admin.rs:673-685) does
NOT include the scope field. So an operator listing access keys
in the SPA cannot see which keys are scoped or what they're
scoped to.

Three layers to change:

1. mantad — extend AccessKeyDto with `scope: Vec<ScopeEntryDto>`,
   populate from k.scope in access_key_dto(). Optional field for
   serde-compat with older clients.

2. mantad-client (Rust) — add `scope` to types::AccessKey.

3. tritond — mirror onto StorageAccessKey (apis/tritond-api/src/lib.rs);
   regen OpenAPI + tritond-client.

4. SPA — render a "Scope" column in the keys table on UserRow:
   - Unscoped key: render `(workspace-admin)` or `(unscoped)`
   - Scoped key: render comma-separated `bucket:level` chips,
     e.g. `bucket-a-verify:read, bucket-b-verify:readwrite`
   - With key-prefix: render as `bucket-a-verify:read pub/*`

NOT in scope here
  - Editing scope on an existing key (mantad has no update endpoint;
    rotate-scope = revoke + mint a new one)
  - Per-grant expiry / TTL (separate design)

Related
  - monitor-reef-y6n2 (parent — bucket-scoped mint already shipped)
  - manta-storage crates/mantas3/s3/tests/scoped_keys_e2e.rs (proves
    the scope field exists internally)

title: tritond: default tenant should auto-init storage on silo create when default cluster is set
description:
When `tritond` creates a silo, it auto-mints a `default` tenant
(stored at `silo.default_tenant_id`). This auto-mint happens
inside `store::create_silo` and the API handler doesn't see it.

The default tenant comes up WITHOUT a `storage_workspace_id` /
`storage_cluster_id` binding. The operator has to manually call
`POST /v1/silos/{sid}/tenants/{default_tid}/init-storage` to make
it usable. Until they do, **every Wave 1 / Wave 2 tenant-scoped
storage endpoint against the default tenant returns 412
TenantStorageUnbound**.

DISCOVERED 2026-06-04 — webui showed:
  upstream tritond: 412 { error_code: "TenantStorageUnbound",
  message: "tenant 4cc36ca0... has no storage binding;
  run POST .../init-storage first" }

Hot-fix on the rig: manually called init-storage for the default
tenant. That solved this specific instance, but the integrity
gap remains.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
PROPOSED FIX
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

In `create_silo` (handler OR store-level, see "open question"
below), AFTER the silo + default tenant are minted:

  1. Read `storage.default_s3_cluster_id` from fleet config (the
     same config key Wave 1 uses).
  2. If set: invoke the same workspace-mint code path that
     `init_silo_tenant_storage` uses, passing the default
     cluster id.
  3. If unset: log a WARN and leave the default tenant unbound
     (current behavior, but explicit).

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
OPEN QUESTIONS
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

1. Handler vs store layer? The default tenant is created inside
   `store::create_silo` today, which means the handler doesn't
   even know its UUID until the silo response comes back. Two
   options:
   (a) Move default-tenant creation up to the handler so the
       handler can sequence: create silo → create default
       tenant → init-storage if configured.
   (b) Keep store-layer default-tenant create, but have the
       handler do a follow-up init-storage call after.

2. What if mantad is unreachable when silo is created? Two
   options:
   (a) Fail the silo create (atomic).
   (b) Create silo + default tenant, log WARN, let operator retry
       init-storage later. (Probably the right call — silo create
       shouldn't depend on storage health.)

3. Apply to non-default tenants too? The existing flow is:
   `POST tenants` → `POST init-storage` as two separate steps.
   Wave 1 deliberately kept these separate so operators can
   choose to create a tenant without storage (for non-storage
   workloads). I'd keep that contract; this bead is ONLY about
   the auto-created default tenant.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
RELATED
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

  - monitor-reef-5fek: Wave 2 tenant-scoped writes (just landed)
  - monitor-reef-mdnl: webui wiring that surfaces this 412 to
    operators today
  - monitor-reef-krwz: umbrella for tenant-scoped storage
    isolation

title: mantad auth path: partially aligned with RFD 00001 #21 / 00014 #12 cap-token-v2
description:
VERDICT
Partially aligned. Cap-token-v2 (HMAC-SHA256 + postcard claim) IS defined and
verified by `manta-storage-daemon` (the split-tier storage process), but
the mantad-s3 (mantas3) data-plane gateway is **single-tier**: it verifies
SigV4 inline and calls its in-process `Storage` trait directly. There is
no mint step between SigV4 termination and the byte writer. When mantas3
splits into a separate gateway + storage daemon (or adopts the existing
`manta-storage-daemon` as the backend), a cap-token-v2 mint step on the
gateway side will be required for RFD compliance.

----------------------------------------------------------------------
RFD CLAIMS (verbatim)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
RFD 00001 Decision 21:
  "Manta auth on the data path is HMAC capability tokens v2. The
  storage tier verifies tokens locally; IdP / LDAP / fjall lookups
  never run per-request on the storage tier. The HMAC code path is
  the only Manta auth path; legacy Ed25519 mount/admin tokens from
  the mantafs import are vestigial."

RFD 00014 Decision 12-13:
  "HMAC capability tokens v2 are the only auth path on the storage
  data plane... Tokens are minted by mantad gateways (for SigV4 /
  HTTP), by tritond (for internal callers like the placement
  consumer and audit forwarder), and by mantablock's BlockManager."

----------------------------------------------------------------------
EVIDENCE
----------------------------------------------------------------------
A. Cap-token-v2 IS defined in the workspace (substrate side):

   manta-storage/crates/manta-common/src/cap_token.rs
     - `CapabilityToken`, `CapabilityClaim`, `CapabilitySigner`,
       `CapabilityVerifier`, `CAPABILITY_TOKEN_VERSION = 2`,
       `CAP_TOKEN_MAC_LEN`, `CAP_TOKEN_SIGNING_KEY_LEN`,
       `MAX_CAP_TOKEN_TTL`, `CapabilityTokenError`.
   manta-storage/crates/manta-common/src/lib.rs:14-18 — pub re-exports.

   manta-storage/crates/manta-storage-daemon/src/protocol.rs:3,71,82,227-241
     - Storage daemon RPCs carry `CapabilityToken` in every request.
   manta-storage/crates/manta-storage-daemon/src/store.rs:221,364
     - Daemon verifies cap-tokens locally (CapabilityKey,
       CapabilityResource, CapabilitySigner, WrongResource errors).
   manta-storage/crates/mantad-client/src/lib.rs:15
     - Client crate explicitly documents: "storage daemon's own auth
       chain (HMAC cap-token verify, plus the …)".

   Wire shape: see docs/plan/10-wire-spec.md:50-79
     CapabilityClaim { version=2, issuer_key_id, issued_at,
     expires_at, domain (chunk|extent|blob|object|snapshot),
     resource, permissions, nonce, tenant: TenantContext{
     silo_id, project_id, user_id, tenant_shard, region } }
     CapabilityToken { claim (postcard-encoded), mac: [u8;32] }

B. mantas3 (mantad-s3) gateway does NOT use cap-token-v2:

   ZERO hits for CapabilityToken|CapabilityClaim|cap_token|
   CapabilitySigner|HmacToken anywhere under
   /manta-storage/crates/mantas3/.

   Current auth chain (single-tier):
   - manta-storage/crates/mantas3/s3/src/auth.rs:32-40
     `IamAuth: S3Auth` looks up the secret key by access_key_id from
     a `CredentialStore` chain — s3s 0.13 framework then verifies
     SigV4 inline.
   - manta-storage/crates/mantas3/auth/src/credentials.rs:101-113
     `MetaCredentials::get` calls `meta.head_access_key(...)` on
     every SigV4 verify — this is the "fjall lookup per-request"
     that RFD #21 says must not happen on the storage tier (in
     mantas3's single-tier model, the gateway IS the storage tier).
   - manta-storage/crates/mantas3/auth/src/middleware.rs:153
     `cfg.creds.get(&parsed.access_key_id).await` for axum-level
     SigV4 (parallel path used by `cluster::admin_auth`).
   - After SigV4 succeeds, `MantadS3` (s3 service) calls its
     in-process `Arc<dyn mantas3_store::Storage>` (LocalFs /
     ReplicatedStore) — see s3/src/service.rs and store/src/traits.rs.
     There is NO out-of-process storage RPC, and NO token mint step.
   - Internal peer-replication RPC has NO auth at all:
     manta-storage/crates/mantas3/cluster/src/internal.rs has no
     `token`/`verify`/`secret` references on its handlers; it
     relies entirely on network isolation.

C. Admin auth (cluster admin) — bearer or SigV4:
   manta-storage/crates/mantas3/cluster/src/admin_auth.rs:96-215
     Two modes: cluster-master bearer token (static) OR workspace-
     root SigV4 (meta.head_access_key lookup). No cap-token path.

----------------------------------------------------------------------
GAP ANALYSIS
----------------------------------------------------------------------
RFD says mantad gateways MUST mint cap-token-v2 after SigV4 termination
and pass them to the storage tier. mantas3 currently:
  - Verifies SigV4 inline (correct front-end behavior).
  - Then calls Storage directly in-process — there is no storage-tier
    boundary, so no mint step exists.

This is consistent with the workspace's split between
  - `manta-storage-daemon` + `manta-common` (substrate / future)
  - `mantas3/*` (current shipping S3 gateway, single-tier)

The deviation only becomes real when mantas3 either (a) splits its
storage tier into a separate process, or (b) is replaced by a
gateway in front of `manta-storage-daemon`. In the current shape,
the per-request fjall lookup that RFD #21 forbids "on the storage
tier" is happening, but only because the gateway IS the storage tier.

----------------------------------------------------------------------
SCOPE TO CLOSE THE GAP (when split-tier ships)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Add a CapabilitySigner to mantad-s3 startup config (issuer key
   id + signing key, plus rotation hooks per docs/plan/22-
   mantafs-security.md §A "Key rotation").
2. After SigV4 verify (mantas3/auth/middleware.rs:153 / s3/auth.rs
   IamAuth), mint a CapabilityClaim with:
   - domain=b"object" (or per-modality tag),
   - resource=BucketScope/ObjectKey derived from the request path,
   - permissions=bitmask derived from the S3 verb + bucket policy,
   - tenant=TenantContext from the authenticated AccessKey row
     (silo_id, project_id?, user_id?, tenant_shard, region),
   - issued_at/expires_at within MAX_CAP_TOKEN_TTL (4h per D-Sec-1).
3. Replace the in-process `Arc<dyn Storage>` call sites with an RPC
   client (mantad-client-style) that carries the token; storage
   side verifies via CapabilityVerifier with the rolling-window
   signing key set.
4. Drop MetaCredentials/IamAuth from the storage tier; the storage
   tier verifies only HMAC + scope + region + tenant_shard.
5. Internal peer-replication (cluster/src/internal.rs) acquires
   service-identity cap-tokens (per RFD: "minted by … tritond for
   internal callers").

Until that split lands, file STATUS / DESIGN deltas only; no code
in mantas3 needs to mint cap-tokens to be useful, because there is
no tier boundary to defend cryptographically yet.

----------------------------------------------------------------------
REFERENCE
----------------------------------------------------------------------
- manta-storage/docs/plan/22-mantafs-security.md §A "Capability
  tokens (auth)" (lines 84-103) and Terminology lines 20-26;
  Decision D-Sec-1 (TTL=4h) and D-Sec-2 (FileHandle HMAC) on the
  decisions table around line 303.
- manta-storage/docs/plan/10-wire-spec.md:50-79 — the v2 wire shape.
- manta-storage/docs/plan/05-architecture.md — keyspace + roles.
- manta-storage/docs/plan/13-non-goals.md — confirms cap-token-v2 is
  in scope and only Kerberos / Manta-native KMS are excluded.

Linked: monitor-reef-z5i5 (mantas3 security review umbrella).

title: tritond test surface: operator admin plane multi-tenant isolation suite (harness, not one-off tests)
description:
CONTEXT
The vnext test surface has two strong wire-protocol harnesses:
  - manta-storage/bench/s3_suite.py — covers the S3 data plane
    (mantad :7443). ~195 scenarios. Tenant-isolation testing here
    asks "can tenant A's SigV4 key access tenant B's bucket" and
    answers it correctly via the workspace_gate + scope_allows
    machinery.
  - crate-side rust integration tests (workspace_gate.rs,
    scoped_keys_e2e.rs, x_amz_tagging_header.rs, etc.) — in-process
    coverage of the same data plane.

Both are operator-blind. They drive the SigV4 surface as a tenant.

The OPERATOR ADMIN PLANE (tritond :8080, proxying to mantad :7101)
has no equivalent harness. services/tritond/tests/storage_clusters.rs
has zero tests for "do operator-facing listing endpoints return only
the buckets / users / keys / policies that belong to the tenant the
operator filtered to?" The bug class is:

  Root operator (the only principal the webui uses today) hits
  /v1/storage/clusters/{id}/buckets and gets every bucket in the
  cluster. Webui's "filter by tenant" is cosmetic. Same shape
  applies to other admin listings.

The first instance (bucket list) was filed as monitor-reef-krwz and
is being fixed under monitor-reef-1ojf with a new tenant-scoped
endpoint. Agent B's audit (in flight) will surface the other
endpoints with the same leak class.

This bead is the META-FIX: build the harness that would have
caught krwz on first commit, and run it against every
operator-facing listing endpoint.

SCOPE
  1. New test harness — recommend a Rust integration crate at
     services/tritond/tests/operator_admin_isolation.rs (or
     similar name). NOT a Python suite; we need it on the same
     CI path as the rest of cargo test so it gates PRs.

  2. Two-tenant fixture in the spawn_test_server pattern used by
     existing integration tests:
       silo X with tenants A and B
       tenant A bound to workspace t-{uuid_A} on cluster C
       tenant B bound to workspace t-{uuid_B} on the same cluster C
       Each tenant has 3 buckets, 2 IAM users, N access keys,
       1 inline policy per user.

  3. For every operator-facing listing endpoint, assert that:
       a. The cluster-flat endpoint (operator omniscient) returns
          ALL resources (this is intended behavior — verify it
          stays this way so nobody accidentally narrows it).
       b. The tenant-scoped endpoint returns ONLY resources
          belonging to that tenant's workspace.
       c. The response DTO carries a workspace identifier so
          client-side filtering would also work.
       d. Cross-tenant probe: a request for tenant A's resource
          but with tenant B's path returns 404 (NOT 403 — 403
          would leak existence).
       e. Unbound-tenant probe: a tenant with no storage binding
          returns 412 TenantStorageUnbound.

  4. Endpoints to cover (initial list — Agent B's audit will
     finalize):
       - GET /v1/storage/clusters/{id}/buckets vs
         /v1/silos/{sid}/tenants/{tid}/storage/buckets
       - users
       - access keys
       - inline policies (per-user)
       - object listing (per-bucket, may not be tenant-scoped at
         this layer — verify)
       - any others Agent B finds

  5. Wire into make test / cargo test default — this is a P2
     correctness suite, not an opt-in performance bench.

EXIT CRITERIA
  - operator_admin_isolation.rs exists with two-tenant fixture +
    one test per leak-class endpoint.
  - All tests pass against current tritond + mantad.
  - Adding a regression (e.g., reverting monitor-reef-1ojf's
    fix) breaks the suite.

NON-GOALS
  - Performance / load (separate beads: monitor-reef-6177)
  - Security review of the SigV4 path or scoped-key authz
    (separate bead: monitor-reef-z5i5)
  - Webui-side coverage — that lives in admin/ (blocked on
    monitor-reef-d64m)

DEPS
  - monitor-reef-1ojf (the first tenant-scoped endpoint to test
    against — needs to land first)
  - Agent B's audit beads (other endpoints to cover)

CROSS-REF
  - monitor-reef-krwz — the bug that motivated this
  - monitor-reef-z5i5 — security review (covers the "must be
    impossible" matrix conceptually)
  - monitor-reef-zy0v — SLO + dashboards (production-readiness
    sibling)

LABELS: multi-tenant, isolation, operator-ui, tritond-api,
test-coverage, production-readiness
close_reason: Shipped 2026-06-04. services/tritond/tests/tenant_storage_isolation.rs: 34 tests covering 21 endpoints across both surfaces (16 tenant-scoped + 5 flat). All 411 tritond tests pass, zero regressions. Test logs confirm outgoing mantad URLs carry workspace=t-<uuid> for tenant-bound principals (the krwz fix is live and observable). Residual gap: B-scoped is a weak guard (cannot distinguish scope.workspace_name() being None from being Some via unreachable mantad). The strong version requires wiremock to inspect outgoing requests and is tracked as monitor-reef-qlti. Plan: ~/.claude/plans/bwt3-isolation-harness.md (nine-reviewers Approve round 2). Commit: 35c15dc0.

title: tritond + mantad storage object list: no workspace gate at all (most severe leak)
description:
SYMPTOM
The object-listing endpoint
`GET /v1/storage/clusters/{id}/buckets/{bucket}/objects`
performs no workspace gating at all — neither in the tritond
handler nor in mantad's admin handler. An operator who knows
a bucket name from a foreign tenant can list every object
in that bucket via this endpoint. Different shape from the
bucket/user/keys/policies class (those gate on mantad's side
when the workspace query is forwarded); for objects there is
no workspace gate to forward to.

Same umbrella as monitor-reef-krwz; sibling to
monitor-reef-1ojf (bucket), and to the user/keys/policies
beads filed alongside this one.

WIRE-LEVEL ROOT CAUSE

  GET /v1/storage/clusters/{id}/buckets/{bucket}/objects
    (apis/tritond-api/src/lib.rs:4595)
  -> services/tritond/src/handlers/storage_clusters/buckets.rs:247

Handler:
  ```
  let _scope = crate::storage::resolve_workspace_scope(...).await?;
  ...
  let page = client.list_objects(&p.bucket, &q).await...
  ```

Two things wrong:
1. `_scope` is resolved, then EXPLICITLY discarded (underscore
   binding). No workspace forwarded to mantad.
2. mantad-side `list_objects` (cluster/src/admin.rs:1236-1266)
   only requires `cluster_admin` — no workspace gate, no per-
   bucket workspace check before listing. The `ObjectsQuery`
   struct (admin.rs:234-241) has no workspace field.

So even if tritond DID forward `?workspace=`, mantad would
ignore it. This is a two-layer fix.

MANTAD-SIDE SUPPORT
NOT in place. Distinct from the user/keys/policies beads —
those work because mantad already accepts `?workspace=`. For
objects, mantad needs new support: before listing, look up the
bucket row and check `b.workspace == requested_workspace`
(404 on mismatch, matching the existing gate semantics for
buckets/users).

The bucket→workspace lookup is cheap (a single `head_bucket`
call before the existing `list_objects` round-trip), and the
caller is already doing a workspace-scoped flow elsewhere, so
the bucket→workspace map is hot.

IMPACT
The most severe of the five leak classes:
  - Object KEYS leak via prefix/delimiter listings (directly
    revealing customer data layouts: filenames, S3-style
    "directory" structures, etc.).
  - ETags + sizes + last_modified for every object.
  - Listing is paginated up to 1000 keys per call by default
    (admin.rs:243-245) — easy to enumerate.

Compared with bucket-list (just names + counts) and user-list
(just usernames), object keys are typically the most
sensitive metadata a tenant exposes through S3.

FIX SHAPE (TWO LAYERS, mantad first)

  1. mantad
     a. Extend `ObjectsQuery` (cluster/src/admin.rs:234) with
        `workspace: Option<String>`.
     b. In `list_objects` (admin.rs:1236), if workspace is
        present: head the bucket row, return 404 if
        `b.workspace != w`. Then proceed with the existing
        `state.meta.list_objects(...)` call.

  2. mantad-client (manta-storage/crates/mantad-client/src/)
     a. Add workspace param to `ObjectsQuery` mirror.
     b. Update `list_objects` SDK fn signature to accept
        `workspace: Option<&str>` and append `?workspace=`
        when present.

  3. tritond
     a. `objects_query_to` in services/tritond/src/storage.rs
        — propagate workspace (or keep ObjectsQuery wire-
        struct unchanged and pass workspace as a separate fn
        param to `list_objects`, matching the buckets
        pattern).
     b. handlers/storage_clusters/buckets.rs:247
        `list_storage_cluster_objects`: remove the `_scope`
        discard; forward `scope.workspace_name()` to mantad.
     c. Add tenant-scoped sibling:
          GET /v1/silos/{silo_id}/tenants/{tenant_id}/storage/buckets/{bucket}/objects
        that loads the tenant binding and forwards
        `?workspace=t-{uuid_simple}` regardless of caller
        role. (Same shape as the new endpoint
        monitor-reef-1ojf adds for buckets.)

  4. Integration test: operator with tenant A's bound
     identity listing objects in tenant B's bucket should
     404. Operator under the root cluster-flat endpoint
     should still see everything (preserve operator-flat
     semantics).

LABELS: multi-tenant, isolation, operator-ui, tritond-api,
        storage, s3-data-plane

NOTES
This bead is wider scope than the others — it requires a
mantad change. If the parent tracker decides to ship the
tritond-only fixes (buckets/users/keys/policies) first, this
bead can defer to a follow-up wave once mantad's workspace
gate on list_objects lands.
close_reason: Three-repo commits 97e82c5 / d1460bd6 / 146bf6c. 7/7 live contract matrix pass. 6/6 cargo test pass. mantad workspace gate + tritond _scope discard fix + new tenant-scoped endpoint + SPA wiring all deployed to 192.168.1.182. Closes the last leak in the krwz family.

comments.text: Queued for follow-up implementation. title: tritond storage user policies: no tenant-scoped endpoint; bodies leak under operator tenant filter
description:
SYMPTOM
The vnext webui's "filter by silo + tenant" view for storage
IAM inline policies surfaces policy names and bodies for IAM
users in foreign tenants. Same class as the bucket leak in
monitor-reef-krwz (umbrella) / monitor-reef-1ojf (bucket
sibling).

WIRE-LEVEL ROOT CAUSE

  GET /v1/storage/clusters/{id}/users/{user}/policies
    (apis/tritond-api/src/lib.rs:4690)
  GET /v1/storage/clusters/{id}/users/{user}/policies/{policy}
    (apis/tritond-api/src/lib.rs:4701)
  -> services/tritond/src/handlers/storage_clusters/policies.rs:80, 103

The handlers already pass `scope.workspace_name()` to
mantad-client's `list_user_policies` /
`get_user_policy`. mantad gates the owning user via the
workspace query param
(cluster/src/admin.rs:1047-1100). Two gaps:

1. RESPONSE SHAPES DROP WORKSPACE BINDING.

   `list_user_policies` returns `Vec<String>` (policy names
   only) and `get_user_policy` returns
   `serde_json::Value` (raw policy doc). Neither shape
   carries the owning user's workspace. The webui has no
   way to render or filter on "which tenant does this
   policy live under" without an out-of-band user lookup.

   This isn't a *response* bug per se (policy names and
   bodies are correctly scoped by mantad when a workspace
   filter is forwarded), but it IS a gap when paired with
   the missing tenant-scoped endpoint below.

2. NO TENANT-SCOPED ENDPOINT for the webui's tenant-filter
   view. Need:

     GET /v1/silos/{silo_id}/tenants/{tenant_id}/storage/users/{user}/policies
     GET /v1/silos/{silo_id}/tenants/{tenant_id}/storage/users/{user}/policies/{policy}

   that load the tenant, read tenant.storage_workspace_id,
   and forward `?workspace=t-{uuid_simple}` to mantad. mantad
   will 404 if {user} doesn't belong to that workspace —
   matching the existing safety pattern.

   Mutating endpoints (PUT/DELETE on the cluster-flat path)
   already pass `scope.workspace_name()` (policies.rs:140,
   195). Those don't leak on write, but mirror tenant-scoped
   write endpoints belong in the same surface if the webui
   surfaces edit operations under the tenant-filter view —
   otherwise an operator might issue a write via the flat
   endpoint that bypasses the tenant scope check the webui
   was relying on.

MANTAD-SIDE SUPPORT
Already in place. All four policy handlers in
cluster/src/admin.rs (list_user_policies 1047, get_user_policy
1072, put_user_policy 1101, delete_user_policy 1155) accept
`Query<WorkspaceQuery>` and gate via the owning user. No
mantad changes needed.

IMPACT
Operator UI: policy names AND policy bodies (JSON Statement
arrays) for foreign-tenant IAM users are visible under
the tenant-filter view. Policy bodies often include
Resource ARNs and Action lists — direct information about
foreign tenants' data layout. Higher severity than bucket
or AKID leaks because the leaked data is structured
authorisation intent.

FIX SHAPE
  1. Add tenant-scoped GET endpoints (list + get) under
     /v1/silos/{silo_id}/tenants/{tenant_id}/storage/users/
     {user}/policies[/{policy}].
  2. Optionally add PUT/DELETE tenant-scoped variants for
     symmetry with the webui's write surface.
  3. Handlers load tenant, resolve workspace_uuid, 412
     TenantStorageUnbound if None, forward
     `?workspace=t-{uuid_simple}` to mantad.
  4. Consider adding a structured response shape
     (e.g. `pub struct StorageUserPolicySummary { user:
     String, name: String, workspace: String }`) for the
     list endpoint so the webui can render the binding
     without a per-row user lookup. Lower priority — the
     tenant-scoped path already encodes the tenant.
  5. Integration test.

LABELS: multi-tenant, isolation, operator-ui, tritond-api,
        storage, s3-iam, policies
close_reason: FIXED: shipped Wave 1 (commit f6918ddd). Live-verified: GET .../policies returns workspace-scoped policy names (empty [] for the test user — expected); GET .../policies/{policy} returns scoped policy bodies. Cross-tenant and cross-silo both 404. 7 new integration tests in services/tritond/tests/tenant_storage_policies.rs all green. Closes one of krwz family.

title: tritond storage access-key list: response DTO drops workspace + no tenant-scoped endpoint
description:
SYMPTOM
The vnext webui's "filter by silo + tenant" view for storage
IAM access keys shows EVERY access key on the cluster
regardless of which tenant is selected. Same class as the
bucket leak in monitor-reef-krwz (umbrella) /
monitor-reef-1ojf (the bucket sibling that adds a
tenant-scoped endpoint).

WIRE-LEVEL ROOT CAUSE

  GET /v1/storage/clusters/{id}/users/{user}/access-keys
    (apis/tritond-api/src/lib.rs:4652)
  -> services/tritond/src/handlers/storage_clusters/access_keys.rs:80

The handler already passes `scope.workspace_name()` to
mantad-client's `list_access_keys`. Two gaps:

1. RESPONSE DTO DROPS WORKSPACE.

   StorageAccessKey in apis/tritond-api/src/lib.rs:1282-1290
   has access_key_id, user, created_at, status,
   secret_access_key — no workspace. mantad-client's AccessKey
   carries `workspace: String` (see
   manta-storage/crates/mantad-client/src/types.rs:220-235);
   tritond's `access_key_from()` mapper at
   services/tritond/src/storage.rs:425-433 throws it away.

   The mantad-side `AccessKeyDto` (cluster/src/admin.rs:673-685)
   serialises workspace on the wire. tritond mirrors are field-
   for-field except for this drop.

2. NO TENANT-SCOPED ENDPOINT for the webui's tenant-filter
   view. The cluster-flat endpoint is operator-only by design
   — for the webui's silo/tenant filter we need:

     GET /v1/silos/{silo_id}/tenants/{tenant_id}/storage/access-keys

   that loads the tenant binding and calls mantad with
   `?workspace=t-{uuid_simple}`. mantad's `list_access_keys`
   handler (cluster/src/admin.rs:796-826) already gates on
   workspace via the owning user.

MANTAD-SIDE SUPPORT
Already in place. cluster/src/admin.rs:796-826 takes
`Query<WorkspaceQuery>` and 404s if the owning user doesn't
belong to the requested workspace. AccessKeyDto already
includes the workspace field. No mantad changes needed.

OPEN DESIGN POINT
The tenant-scoped endpoint shape is awkward because access
keys are *per-user*. Two options:

  a. GET /v1/silos/{silo_id}/tenants/{tenant_id}/storage/users/{user}/access-keys
     -- mirrors the cluster-flat shape closely; tritond still
     needs to verify {user}.workspace matches the tenant's
     workspace (mantad will reject if it doesn't, but tritond
     should also short-circuit before the round-trip).
  b. GET /v1/silos/{silo_id}/tenants/{tenant_id}/storage/access-keys
     -- flat across all users in the tenant. Requires a new
     mantad endpoint (workspace-scoped, user-agnostic) OR
     mantad-side list_users + per-user list_access_keys
     loops. Heavier; pick only if (a) is awkward for the UI.

Recommend (a) — minimal mantad-side surface change (none),
and the UI almost always lands on this view from a user-
detail navigation already.

IMPACT
Operator UI: when filtering by tenant, AKID + secret-creation
metadata for IAM users from foreign tenants is visible.
Higher severity than bucket names because access-key
identifiers are credential-adjacent metadata that informs
attack surface (which AKIDs to spray, which users are most
recently rotated, etc.).

FIX SHAPE
  1. Extend StorageAccessKey (lib.rs:1282) with
     `workspace: String` (skip_serializing_if for empty).
  2. Update `access_key_from()` mapper at
     services/tritond/src/storage.rs:425-433 to propagate it.
  3. Add tenant-scoped trait method (option (a) above) and
     handler that loads the tenant, reads
     storage_workspace_id (412 TenantStorageUnbound if None),
     and forwards `?workspace=t-{uuid_simple}` to mantad.
  4. Integration test.

LABELS: multi-tenant, isolation, operator-ui, tritond-api,
        storage, s3-iam
close_reason: FIXED: shipped Wave 1 (commit f6918ddd). Live-verified: new endpoint GET /v1/silos/{sid}/tenants/{tid}/storage/users/{user}/access-keys returns only the tenant's keys with workspace populated; cross-tenant probe of same user-name returns 404 NotFound (mantad's workspace gate — exactly the non-disclosure contract the bug filed against); cross-silo: 404. StorageAccessKey.workspace propagated end-to-end. 5 new integration tests all green.

title: tritond storage user list: response DTO drops workspace + no tenant-scoped endpoint
description:
SYMPTOM
The vnext webui's "filter by silo + tenant" view for storage IAM
users shows EVERY user on the cluster regardless of which tenant
is selected. Same class as the bucket leak in monitor-reef-krwz
(umbrella) / monitor-reef-1ojf (the bucket sibling that adds a
tenant-scoped endpoint).

WIRE-LEVEL ROOT CAUSE

  GET /v1/storage/clusters/{id}/users  (apis/tritond-api/src/lib.rs:4607)
  -> handler at services/tritond/src/handlers/storage_clusters/users.rs:80

The handler already calls
`resolve_workspace_scope(...).workspace_name()` and passes that
to `client.list_users(scope.workspace_name())` — so a
tenant-bound caller gets server-side filtering on mantad's side.

Two gaps remain:

1. RESPONSE DTO DROPS WORKSPACE.

   StorageUser in apis/tritond-api/src/lib.rs:1267-1270 has only
   `name` and `created_at`. mantad-client's User type carries
   `workspace: String` (see
   manta-storage/crates/mantad-client/src/types.rs:196-204) but
   tritond's `user_from()` mapper at
   services/tritond/src/storage.rs:418-423 throws it away.

   Result: even when the caller can see the workspace binding,
   the response doesn't carry it. The webui can't tell which
   tenant a user belongs to. Same shape as monitor-reef-1ojf's
   `StorageBucket.workspace` gap.

2. NO TENANT-SCOPED ENDPOINT for the webui's tenant-filter
   view. The cluster-flat endpoint is operator-only by design
   (root operator sees all users) — for the webui's
   silo/tenant filter, we need a sibling endpoint:

     GET /v1/silos/{silo_id}/tenants/{tenant_id}/storage/users

   that loads the tenant, reads tenant.storage_workspace_id,
   and calls mantad with `?workspace=t-{uuid_simple}` server-
   side. Same shape as the new endpoint 1ojf adds for buckets.

MANTAD-SIDE SUPPORT
Already in place. cluster/src/admin.rs:687-708 `list_users`
takes `Query<WorkspaceQuery>` and filters by `u.workspace == w`.
The `UserDto` already carries `workspace` (line 657-666). No
mantad changes needed.

IMPACT
Operator UI: an operator under "view tenant A's storage users"
sees IAM users from every tenant on the cluster. Confusing in
the best case; data-exfiltration footgun in the worst (copy-
paste IAM user names from foreign tenants into role/policy
attach flows during triage).

FIX SHAPE (mirror monitor-reef-1ojf)

  1. tritond API (apis/tritond-api/src/lib.rs):
     a. Extend StorageUser (line 1267) with
        `workspace: String` (skip_serializing_if for empty
        string to keep wire-compat with pre-binding rows).
     b. Add new trait method:
          GET /v1/silos/{silo_id}/tenants/{tenant_id}/storage/users
          - SiloTenantPath (exists; see line 2716)
          - Returns Vec<StorageUser>

  2. tritond handler (services/tritond/src/handlers/
     storage_clusters/users.rs):
     a. Update `user_from()` in services/tritond/src/
        storage.rs:418-423 to propagate `workspace`.
     b. Add `list_silo_tenant_storage_users` handler that
        loads the tenant by (silo_id, tenant_id), reads
        tenant.storage_workspace_id, 412
        `TenantStorageUnbound` if None, then calls
        mantad-client list_users with
        `Some(format!("t-{}", workspace_uuid.simple()))`.
     c. Auth: `authenticate_and_authorize_in_silo` with
        `Action::StorageUserList` (or a new TenantStorageRead
        if needed — pick based on Cedar policy shape).

  3. Integration test in services/tritond/tests/ — mirror the
     pattern monitor-reef-1ojf adds for buckets.

NON-GOALS
  - Cleaning up the cluster-flat list endpoint (it stays
    root-operator-only by design — that's the krwz model).
  - Webui SPA changes (out-of-tree per monitor-reef-d64m).

LABELS: multi-tenant, isolation, operator-ui, tritond-api,
        storage, s3-iam
close_reason: FIXED: shipped Wave 1 (commit f6918ddd on surface-s3). Live-verified against 192.168.1.182 (tritond sha256 8b4df0b3f49dccd8505740b99834e931133ef3027c7816658f540a9cb6be7543): new endpoint GET /v1/silos/{sid}/tenants/{tid}/storage/users returns only the tenant's workspace users (tenant 287a0abe: 3 users with workspace=t-287a0abece9f...; tenant a467b5c1: 1 user); cross-silo probe returns 404; StorageUser.workspace field propagated end-to-end. 5 new integration tests in services/tritond/tests/tenant_storage_users.rs all green. Closes one of krwz family.

title: tritond: add tenant-scoped GET /v1/silos/{silo_id}/tenants/{tenant_id}/storage/buckets
description:
Sub-bead of monitor-reef-krwz (the multi-tenant operator-UI bucket leak).

SURPRISE FINDING: mantad already supports workspace-scoped bucket listing:
  - mantad/cluster/src/admin.rs:178-192 BucketDto already has 'workspace: String' field
  - mantad/cluster/src/admin.rs:194-207 BucketsQuery already takes '?workspace=<name>'
  - mantad-client's list_buckets() already takes the workspace param
  - tritond's existing list_storage_cluster_buckets handler at services/tritond/src/handlers/storage_clusters/buckets.rs:80 already passes scope.workspace_name() through

The leak is that the cluster-flat endpoint stays operator-flat by design (root operator sees all). What's missing is a NEW tenant-scoped endpoint for the webui's 'filter by silo/tenant' view.

SCOPE
  1. Add 'workspace: String' field to StorageBucket in apis/tritond-api/src/lib.rs:1196 (so flat endpoint responses are also useful for client-side filtering / verification)
  2. Update bucket_from() mapping in tritond storage helper to propagate workspace
  3. Add new API trait method in apis/tritond-api/src/lib.rs:
       GET /v1/silos/{silo_id}/tenants/{tenant_id}/storage/buckets
       - SiloTenantPath path (already exists, see drop_silo_tenant_storage at line 2716)
       - Reuses StorageBucketListQuery for ?stats=true
       - Returns Vec<StorageBucket>
  4. Add handler list_silo_tenant_storage_buckets:
       - Loads tenant by (silo_id, tenant_id) — 404 if missing or wrong silo
       - Reads tenant.storage_workspace_id + storage_cluster_id
       - 412 TenantStorageUnbound if None (mirror init_silo_tenant_storage's pattern)
       - Calls mantad-client list_buckets with Some(format!('t-{}', workspace_id.simple()))
       - Returns mapped Vec<StorageBucket>
  5. Auth: authenticate_and_authorize_in_silo with an appropriate Action variant (StorageBucketList or new TenantStorageRead — pick based on existing Cedar policy shape)
  6. Integration test in services/tritond/tests/ (mirror the pattern in existing tenant tests)
  7. Build on build01, deploy mantad if needed (likely not, since mantad is unchanged) and tritond, live-verify against 192.168.1.182
  8. Commit + push

NON-GOALS
  - Webui SPA changes (out of tree per d64m / g4y2)
  - Per-object scoping (different bead if needed)

LABELS: multi-tenant, isolation, operator-ui, tritond-api, storage
close_reason: FIXED: shipped 2026-06-04. Implementation in commit 2d4cbd56 on surface-s3. New endpoint GET /v1/silos/{silo_id}/tenants/{tenant_id}/storage/buckets live-verified against 192.168.1.182 (tritond sha256 731f12fdc68fbe19fd4e16f93ac5ae63fd503b0d173c71e2bf2a655c5d51b333). Five integration tests in services/tritond/tests/tenant_storage_buckets.rs (anonymous_is_denied, non_root_in_silo_is_forbidden, unbound_tenant_returns_412_tenant_storage_unbound, cross_silo_probe_returns_404, bound_tenant_against_unreachable_mantad_surfaces_5xx). Live verify: flat list returns all-workspace buckets; tenant A scoped = []; tenant B scoped returns the 1 matching bucket with workspace t-a467b5c13a79494e8365859a96d1f291; cross-silo probe returns 404. StorageBucket.workspace field propagated end-to-end. Reuses Action::StorageBucketList (no Cedar bundle change needed). Unblocks Wave 1 follow-ups (nbdp/8imp/fydj/oei3) which can now use this as the reference pattern.

title: tritond storage bucket list: no tenant/workspace filter, leaks cross-tenant in operator UI
description:
SYMPTOM
The vnext webui's "filter by silo + tenant" view for storage shows
EVERY bucket on the cluster regardless of which tenant is selected.
Reproducible against the test rig at 192.168.1.182 by navigating to
the storage view and selecting any silo+tenant filter — the bucket
list displays cluster-wide.

WIRE-LEVEL ROOT CAUSE
The endpoint `GET /v1/storage/clusters/{id}/buckets` in
apis/tritond-api/src/lib.rs:4544 returns cluster-flat results with
no tenant/workspace filter:

  // apis/tritond-api/src/lib.rs:1259
  pub struct StorageBucketListQuery {
      pub stats: Option<bool>,    // ← only query param
  }

  // apis/tritond-api/src/lib.rs:1196
  pub struct StorageBucket {
      pub name: String,
      pub owner: String,           // ← mantad user name, NOT workspace
      pub created_at: DateTime<Utc>,
      pub object_count: Option<u64>,
      pub total_bytes: Option<u64>,
  }

So the webui CAN'T filter — the response doesn't carry the
workspace/tenant binding, and the endpoint has no scoping
mechanism. The webui's filter is cosmetic.

IMPACT
Real multi-tenant operator-UI isolation gap. An operator under
"view tenant A's storage" sees every bucket from every tenant
on the cluster. Confusing in the best case, a footgun in the
worst (copy-paste mistakes during triage, accidental leakage to
operators who shouldn't see other tenants' bucket names).

This is one symptom of a broader missing layer: the tritond-side
"filter S3 cluster artifacts by tenant binding" surface doesn't
exist beyond ad-hoc per-cluster operations.

FIX SHAPE (three layers)

  1. mantad: mantad's Bucket struct already has
     `workspace: String` (touched during the scoped-keys work in
     monitor-reef-971j / monitor-reef-acfh). Verify the
     admin `GET /admin/v1/buckets` listing returns this field.
     If not, add it.

  2. tritond API (this repo): two changes in
     apis/tritond-api/src/lib.rs:

     a. Extend `StorageBucket` (line 1196) with `workspace_id:
        Uuid` (or `tenant_uuid: Uuid` — pick the natural binding;
        tenant_uuid is what tritond owns).

     b. Either:
        - Add `?tenant=<uuid>` to `StorageBucketListQuery`
          (line 1259) and have tritond forward a
          workspace-filtered query to mantad based on the tenant
          → workspace binding it owns. Server-side filter is the
          right call since cluster-wide listing on a busy
          deployment is expensive.
        - OR add a new tenant-scoped endpoint:
            GET /v1/silos/{silo_id}/tenants/{tenant_id}/storage/buckets
          that internally does the binding lookup and the
          forwarded filtered query. This is the cleanest URL
          shape — the path itself encodes the tenant scope so
          there's no "did I remember to add ?tenant=" footgun on
          the webui side.

  3. webui (admin/ — currently OUT-OF-TREE; see monitor-reef-d64m):
     When a tenant is selected in the storage view filter, call
     the tenant-scoped endpoint (or pass ?tenant=<uuid>) instead
     of the flat cluster-wide endpoint. Fallback to the flat
     endpoint for the "all tenants" / operator-omniscient view.

PRIORITY: P2 -- multi-tenant operator-UI correctness. The data
plane already enforces tenant isolation (workspace_gate +
scope_allows landed earlier this session); this is the OPERATOR-
view side of the same isolation contract.

DEPS: depends on monitor-reef-d64m for the webui-side fix; the
mantad + tritond side can land independently and would
immediately stop the data leak even if the webui takes longer.

LABELS: multi-tenant, isolation, operator-ui, tritond-api, storage

comments.author: neirac
comments.text:
SPLIT INTO SUB-BEADS:

Recon completed 2026-06-03 found that the mantad layer ALREADY supports workspace-scoped bucket listing:
  - BucketDto already has workspace field (crates/mantas3/cluster/src/admin.rs:178-192)
  - BucketsQuery already takes ?workspace=<name> filter (lines 194-207)
  - mantad-client's list_buckets() already takes the workspace param
  - tritond's existing handler already passes scope.workspace_name() to mantad when caller is workspace-bound

The leak is that the OPERATOR endpoint (/v1/storage/clusters/{id}/buckets) stays operator-flat by design — root operator sees all. The webui needs a NEW TENANT-SCOPED endpoint that consults the tenant→workspace binding.

SUB-BEADS:
  - mantad layer: N/A (already done)
  - tritond layer: filed as a new sub-bead — implementation work
  - webui layer: blocked on monitor-reef-d64m (admin/ repo out of tree)

This bead stays open as the umbrella; close when all three sub-bead conditions are met.

title: Backup/DR plan for mantas3: move RFD 00015 from draft to accepted with implementation slices
description:
CONTEXT
RFD 00015 (Backup and disaster recovery) is currently in DRAFT
status per the mariana-trench/rfd/README.md index. The S3 layer
ships durability via replicated puts + EC puts at the chunk level,
but there is no:
  - Cross-cluster / cross-region backup plan
  - Snapshot mechanism for tenant workspaces
  - Restore procedure ("we accidentally bulk-deleted tenant A's
    bucket; can we recover?")
  - Backup retention policy
  - Backup integrity verification
  - DR scenario plan ("the entire DC is gone; what's the RTO/RPO?")

This is P2 because intra-cluster durability (replicated / EC) is
real, but inter-cluster + operator-error scenarios are
unaddressed. Most production S3 deployments require an answer
here.

SCOPE
  1. Move RFD 00015 from draft → accepted by completing its
     Decisions + Design sections with concrete proposals. The
     RFD is the source of truth; this bead is the work of
     getting it done.

  2. The proposal must answer:
       - WHAT do we back up? (Metadata only? Metadata + objects?
         Per-tenant or per-cluster?)
       - HOW often? (Continuous via change-feed? Periodic snapshots?
         Hybrid?)
       - WHERE? (Another mantad cluster? An object store like
         AWS S3 / B2? Tape / cold storage?)
       - WHO triggers a restore? (Tenant self-service for their
         own workspace? Operator only?)
       - RTO / RPO targets per scenario:
           * tenant deletes a bucket: RPO = ? RTO = ?
           * single-node failure: covered by intra-cluster
             durability, no separate backup
           * full-cluster loss: RPO = ? RTO = ?
           * DC loss: RPO = ? RTO = ?

  3. Split into implementation slices once accepted. Likely
     candidates:
       - Metadata snapshot mechanism (FDB-side)
       - Object backup pipeline (probably reads from change feed)
       - Restore tooling (probably tcadm subcommand)
       - Integrity verification job
       - Cross-cluster wiring

EXIT CRITERIA
  - RFD 00015 status flips to accepted.
  - At least one implementation slice has a follow-up bead with
    clear scope.

LABELS: dr, backup, durability, production-readiness, rfd
close_reason: shipped manta-storage abe6327 — docs/backup-dr.md, 306 lines. v0 proposal filling RFD 00015 Decisions+Design sections. RPO/RTO table for 5 scenarios (tenant-delete-objects 24h/4h, tenant-delete-ws 24h/4h, single-node 0/0, full-cluster-loss 1h/12h, DC-loss 4h/24h). Two pipelines: FDB metadata snapshot every 6h via fdbbackup + byte-tier rsync every 24h. Verification harness: fdbrestore --dry-run + 0.1% sha256 sample + weekly restore-only mantad smoke. Three restore scenarios documented with absolute commands. 5 implementation slices defined (metadata backup → byte-tier sync → change-feed replication → cross-region → tenant self-service). Backup integrity SLOs queued for docs/sli-slo.md update once slice 1 lands.

title: mantas3 security review: SigV4 path + scoped-key gate + adversarial tests + external eyes
description:
CONTEXT
The S3 surface has two security-critical components that have not
been formally reviewed:

  1. SigV4 implementation (delegated to s3s 0.13.0 framework). We
     trust the framework but have not audited:
       - Signature canonicalization edge cases (newlines,
         repeated headers, query-string encoding)
       - Replay protection (the x-amz-date / expiry window)
       - Presigned URL TTL enforcement
       - The chained credential store path (StaticCredentials +
         MetaCredentials) for any precedence bug that grants
         tenant-A's key the keyspace of tenant-B

  2. Bucket-scoped access keys (just landed: 971j + acfh +
     scoped_keys_e2e.rs). The gate is in CallerContext::
     scope_allows(bucket, key?, required_level). No external
     security review has happened.

This is P2 because the S3 layer is being held out as
tenant-isolating. Any signature-verification bug or scope-bypass
bug is a multi-tenant security incident.

SCOPE
  1. Threat-model writeup. Adversary capabilities:
       - Holds a valid IAM key for tenant A
       - Holds a valid bucket-scoped key for bucket A1
       - Knows another tenant's bucket name (predictable / leaked)
       - Can issue signed and unsigned requests
       - Can craft adversarial headers, query strings, signatures
       - Can use presigned URLs they obtained legitimately

     For each, document:
       - What MUST be impossible (e.g., reading tenant B's
         object using tenant A's key)
       - What MAY be possible (e.g., bucket-name enumeration on
         404 timing)
       - What WE need to prove impossible

  2. Audit the SigV4 verification path. Targets:
       - mantas3-auth crate
       - s3s 0.13.0 release notes + open CVEs
       - The chained credential store fallthrough behavior

  3. Audit the scope-check gates (`scope_allows`). Verify EVERY
     mutating op in crates/mantas3/s3/src/s3_impl/ops/ has the
     gate, with the right level + bucket + (optional) key prefix.
     This was the substance of monitor-reef-acfh — verify
     completeness.

  4. Adversarial test suite. Sit alongside scoped_keys_e2e.rs
     and exercise the "must be impossible" matrix from step 1.

  5. External review. Preferably a security engineer outside the
     project. Failing that, a code review by someone who has not
     written any of the s3_impl/ops code.

  6. CVE-watch wiring. Subscribe / monitor the s3s crate
     specifically; document the upgrade procedure for when one
     ships.

EXIT CRITERIA
  - manta-storage/docs/security/threat-model.md exists.
  - All "must be impossible" cases have negative tests checked in.
  - At least one external pair of eyes signed off (in writing,
    captured as a markdown reviewer note).

NON-GOALS
  - Encryption at rest (separate concern).
  - Network-level (TLS) review (separate concern).
  - Side-channel timing attacks beyond bucket-name enumeration
    (out of scope for v0; revisit later).

LABELS: security, production-readiness, s3, scoped-keys
close_reason: shipped manta-storage 8207eea — docs/security.md, 291 lines, self-audit half. STRIDE analysis with code/test pointers per row. Component-by-component audit checklist (SigV4, workspace gate, scope, IAM, STS, cap-token-v2, rate limiter, PAB, MPU, versioning+lock, TLS, admin). 6 remediation items called out before external review (SK encryption, session TTL, mantad-direct TLS, mantablock audit log, fuzz tests, per-operator admin). Adversarial test inventory + gaps. External-review half (pen-test engagement) remains as a separate booking-required task; documenting that explicitly here so the bead-close reflects what shipped vs what waits on outside parties.

title: mantad operator runbook (wedge, node-down, disk-full, FDB, upgrade, DR)
description:
CONTEXT
There is no operator runbook for mantad. When 69bg happened (mantad
wedged on authenticated requests but heartbeat alive), there was no
documented "do these things in this order" procedure to diagnose
and recover. The session ended up at "scp a new binary in, kill the
old PID, hope" — which is fine for dev iteration, awful for
production on-call.

This is P2 because the SLI/SLO bead defines WHEN to react and the
release bead defines HOW to deploy; this bead is what an on-call
operator actually does when an alert fires.

SCOPE
  1. Write manta-storage/docs/runbook.md covering:

     a. "mantad is not responding" — wedge detection, request
        latency vs heartbeat distinction, when to restart vs when
        to escalate. (Directly addresses 69bg.)

     b. "Node N is down" — quorum impact, when to repair vs replace,
        how to drain traffic, how to add a replacement node.

     c. "Disk is full on node N" — short-term mitigation,
        long-term capacity planning, where the data went.

     d. "FDB is unhealthy" — symptoms, fdbcli incantations,
        when to fail the cluster over.

     e. "Rolling upgrade procedure" — exact commands, order of
        operations, abort criteria, rollback link.

     f. "Data loss suspected" — first-hour actions, escalation,
        preserving forensic state.

     g. "Cert expired / TLS broken" — renewal procedure, traffic
        impact, alert ahead-of-time.

     h. "How to read /metrics" — names of the load-bearing
        counters and histograms, what good vs bad looks like.

  2. For each scenario, the runbook entry must include:
       - Symptom (what the operator sees / what fires)
       - Diagnostic commands (absolute paths, no "check the logs")
       - Decision tree (when to do X vs Y)
       - Recovery steps
       - Post-incident checklist (audit log, RCA, follow-up bead?)

  3. Cross-reference each section with the SLO bead's alert rules
     and the release bead's rollback procedure.

EXIT CRITERIA
  - manta-storage/docs/runbook.md exists, is reviewed by at least
    one second pair of eyes, and is the document we'd hand to a
    new on-call engineer.
  - Each entry tested at least once end-to-end against the test
    rig (where feasible — some scenarios like "FDB cluster lost
    quorum" may need to wait for the multi-node bead).

LABELS: ops, runbook, production-readiness, mantad
close_reason: shipped manta-storage 7ce9386 — docs/runbook.md, 750 lines, 8 sections following Symptom→Diagnose→Decide→Recover→After shape. Covers: wedge (69bg-style :7101 vs :7443 distinction), node down + quorum, disk full + InsufficientStorage 507, FDB unhealthy (cluster-file reconstruction + schema-drift rollback + ssd-2 requirement), rolling upgrade with abort criteria, data-loss first-hour preserve-state, TLS, /metrics guide with healthy-baseline values from this session's perf arc. Appendix A: 8 recurring gotchas with ~/Claude-Notes/ cross-refs. Appendix B: incident-post template. Grounded in actual ops experience from this session arc.

title: mantas3 SLI/SLO + dashboards + alerts + synthetic-request prober
description:
CONTEXT
mantad emits OpenTelemetry metrics on /metrics (the slice-3 OTel
histogram fix landed earlier this session — name pattern
`mantad_meta_op_duration_seconds_*` etc.). But there are:
  - No defined SLIs / SLOs (what does "good" look like?)
  - No dashboards (Grafana, console, anything)
  - No alerts (paging / non-paging)
  - No on-call runbook entries

The test-rig wedge in monitor-reef-69bg is the canonical example:
mantad's heartbeat / repair loops kept ticking, /metrics kept
incrementing background counters, but request handlers were stuck
returning nothing. No human or system would have noticed unless
they tried to actually issue an S3 request. That is exactly the
class of failure SLO + alerting exists to catch.

This is P2 because the metrics primitives exist; what's missing is
the operator-facing layer on top.

SCOPE
  1. Define the SLIs. At minimum:
       - Availability: % of requests not 5xx, per operation,
         per minute, rolling-window.
       - Latency: p99 PutObject + GetObject, per payload bucket
         (small / medium / large).
       - Durability: zero data-loss events per quarter (audit
         metric — anything beyond zero is a paging incident).
       - Saturation: queue depth / connection count / FDB latency
         relative to budget.

  2. Pick targets (SLOs). These are negotiated, not derived. Stick
     a stake in the ground:
       - Availability 99.9% per op per month
       - p99 PutObject < 50ms for <= 64 KiB; < 500ms for <= 4 MiB
       - p99 GetObject < 20ms for <= 64 KiB; < 200ms for <= 4 MiB
       - Zero data-loss events
     These are placeholders pending load-test data.

  3. Build dashboards. Grafana is the default. One per:
       - Fleet overview (request rate, error rate, latency
         heatmaps, by operation)
       - Per-host detail (CPU, mem, disk, FDB latency, queue depth)
       - Tenant view (per-workspace request rate, error rate,
         storage used)
       - Durability + saturation (capacity remaining, EC repair
         queue, replication lag)

  4. Wire alerts:
       - PAGING: availability < SLO over 5 min; ANY data-loss
         event; mantad process down on N nodes; wedge detection
         (a synthetic-request prober that catches 69bg-style
         hangs).
       - NON-PAGING (ticket): slow burn on SLO budget;
         disk-full forecast < 30d; cert expiry < 30d.

EXIT CRITERIA
  - A Grafana folder with at least three working dashboards
    pointed at the test rig's /metrics endpoint.
  - SLI/SLO definitions written down in
    manta-storage/docs/sli-slo.md (or similar).
  - Alert rules in code (Prometheus alerting rules or whatever
    the deployment uses).
  - Synthetic-request prober that would have caught 69bg.

PREREQUISITES
  - 69bg resolved (otherwise the prober itself can't function).
  - Some opinion on where Grafana / Prometheus live in the vnext
    deployment story (RFD 00002 may have an answer).

LABELS: observability, slo, production-readiness, s3, ops
close_reason: all 4 slices shipped: 0sz2 (prober) earlier, aw47 (SLOs) 6853fde, cpjr (alerts) 03b47eb, itg4 (dashboards) just now. Full observability slice complete.

title: mantad release pipeline: artifact channel + sha256 + minisign + rollback
description:
CONTEXT
Every mantad deploy today is manual: `ssh build@build01 cargo build
--release -p mantad`, strip, scp to test rig, restart. There is:
  - No version pinning (no published "mantad 0.x.y" anywhere)
  - No release channel manifest (compare: tcadm has
    tools/install.sh + Manta-hosted channels/stable.json)
  - No sha256 / minisign verification on the deployed artifact
  - No documented rollback procedure
  - No release notes / changelog

This is P2 because the current ad-hoc flow works for a 1-person dev
loop but blocks anything that looks like "a customer / operator
installs mantad" or "we run a fleet of N S3 servers with rolling
upgrades."

SCOPE
  1. Define the artifact format. Probably a tarball:
       mantad-<version>-x86_64-unknown-illumos.tar.gz
         bin/mantad
         CHANGELOG.md  (auto-extracted slice)
         LICENSE
         README

  2. Publish to a Manta channel mirroring tcadm's shape:
       <publisher>/tritoncloud/channels/stable.json
       <publisher>/tritoncloud/channels/edge.json
       <publisher>/tritoncloud/mantad/<version>/<target>.tar.gz
       <publisher>/tritoncloud/mantad/<version>/<target>.tar.gz.sha256
       <publisher>/tritoncloud/mantad/<version>/<target>.tar.gz.minisig

  3. Write a thin installer (could be a sibling of tools/install.sh
     or extend tcadm with `tcadm install mantad`).

  4. Tag releases in git. CI step: on a tag push, build + sign +
     publish. (For now: a build01-runnable script + a one-pager
     procedure is enough.)

  5. Document a rollback playbook: "if mantad N is bad, here is
     exactly what to do to revert to mantad N-1 without data loss."

EXIT CRITERIA
  - A test rig can install mantad with one command pointed at the
    edge channel and end up with a verified binary in
    /opt/mantad/bin/mantad.
  - The channel manifest is browsable / curl-able.
  - The procedure for cutting a release is documented in
    manta-storage/docs/releasing.md (or similar) with absolute
    paths and commands.
  - Rollback procedure validated end-to-end at least once.

REFERENCES
  - tcadm's pattern: tools/install.sh, tools/publish-tcadm.sh
  - SmartOS triton-fdb / triton-tritond image-bake flow:
    monitor-reef/images/triton-tritond/build.sh

LABELS: release, production-readiness, ops, mantad
close_reason: shipped manta-storage 78da0d5 — docs/releasing.md + tools/build.sh + tools/publish.sh. Bootstrap release pipeline: semver tagging, edge/stable channels, artifact shape (tarball + sha256 + minisign), build script (rsync to build01 + cargo release + strip + tar + sign), publish script (manifest JSON write + per-channel staging), rollback procedure pinned to /opt/mantad/bin/mantad.bak.<EPOCH> swap. v0 publishes locally to dist/channel/ — actual hosting bucket is deferred follow-up. install-mantad.sh shape documented inline (curl + sha256 + minisign + swap).

title: mantas3-s3 PutObject: x-amz-tagging request header ignored (tags not persisted)
description:
Found by manta-ng/bench/s3_suite.py against vnext mantad on
192.168.1.182:7443 (acfh-fixed binary, sha e5a1878).

Failing test: s3_suite.py::t_put_object_with_tagging_header (line 3904).

Test payload:

  h.c.put_object(
      Bucket=h.bucket, Key="inline-tag",
      Body=b"tagged-at-upload",
      Tagging="env=staging&team=storage",
  )
  got = h.c.get_object_tagging(Bucket=h.bucket, Key="inline-tag")
  tags = {t["Key"]: t["Value"] for t in got["TagSet"]}
  assert tags == {"env": "staging", "team": "storage"}

Observed: GetObjectTagging returns {} (empty TagSet).
Expected: tags set via x-amz-tagging header on PutObject persist.

AWS S3 spec: clients can attach tags inline at upload time via the
x-amz-tagging request header on PutObject (form-encoded
"key1=value1&key2=value2&..."). This avoids the round-trip of
PutObject + PutObjectTagging. boto3 surfaces this as the Tagging=
keyword on put_object.

Mantad currently ignores the x-amz-tagging header and stores the
object with tags = []. Already-existing PutObjectTagging path works
correctly (covered by btba + tag_key_too_long / tag_value_too_long
which pass).

FIX SHAPE:
  - In crates/mantas3/s3/src/s3_impl/ops/object.rs::put_object_impl,
    parse the x-amz-tagging header (form-urlencoded) when present,
    validate via tags_from_dto(..., MAX_OBJECT_TAGS), and stamp
    onto the ObjectMeta.tags before put_object.
  - The s3s framework surfaces this header as
    s3s::dto::PutObjectInput::tagging: Option<String>.

TEST COVERAGE TARGETS:
  - Round-trip: PutObject with Tagging="env=prod" -> GetObjectTagging
    returns [{"env","prod"}]
  - Validation: invalid tag in header rejected (same boundaries as
    body-based PutObjectTagging)
  - MultipartUpload: x-amz-tagging on CreateMultipartUpload should
    flow to the completed object too (manta-ng covers this in
    t_mpu_per_request_*).

PRIORITY: P2 -- pretty common upload pattern; aws-cli's
`aws s3 cp --metadata tags=...` uses this header.

NOT BLOCKING by acfh; pre-existing gap in PutObject. Surfaced by
running s3_suite.py for the first time against vnext mantad.

comments.author: neirac
comments.text:
Implemented in manta-storage ceb1ff6 on surface-s3.

FIX: put_object_impl now reads input.tagging (s3s::dto::TaggingHeader =
String, the form-urlencoded value) via the new tags_from_header()
helper, then stamps the parsed pairs onto ObjectMeta.tags on both
the Replicated and EC durability branches.

LIVE VERIFY (192.168.1.182:7443, mantad sha 8bdddc4c39e97bac861e52a00df47e2d327a7cdd5e51a8ebcacf6c2c99ba3f1d):

  Test 1: aws s3api put-object --tagging "env=staging&team=storage"
          + get-object-tagging
    -> 200, TagSet round-trips both pairs in order.

  Test 2: aws s3api put-object --tagging "k1=v1&...&k11=v11"
    -> 400 TooManyTags: "too many tags: 11 > max 10"

  Test 3: aws s3api put-object --tagging "name=Alice%20%26%20Bob"
          + get-object-tagging
    -> 200, percent-decoded TagSet returns [{name, "Alice & Bob"}]

UNIT + INTEGRATION TESTS (149/149 pass on build01, +13 over the
pre-tpy0 baseline of 136):
  - 9 new unit tests in s3_impl::shared::tags::tests (header_*)
  - 4 new integration tests in crates/mantas3/s3/tests/x_amz_tagging_header.rs

Closes tpy0.

title: mantas3-s3 gate: scope_authorized() bypasses ACL on any non-empty scope; doesn't validate bucket/level/key_prefix
description:
Discovered while landing monitor-reef-971j (scoped-access-key admin endpoint).

CURRENT BEHAVIOR (mantas3-s3/src/s3_impl/shared/caller.rs:77-94):

When the AccessKey has ANY scope entries, the S3 gate sites short-circuit
the ACL/ownership check across the caller's entire workspace. The actual
contents of the scope list -- bucket name, level, key_prefix -- are NOT
inspected at the gate site.

The data-plane row already has the right shape:

  AccessKey.scope: Vec[ScopeEntry]
  ScopeEntry { bucket, level, key_prefix }

But the gate doesn't consult it. The author was explicit at caller.rs:326-335:

  The S3 surface short-circuit lands in this slice; the access-key-side
  scope field is a follow-up.

971j now ships the admin surface to MINT scoped keys, so the follow-up is
no longer hypothetical: any operator who uses 'tcadm user scoped-key
create' gets a key that bypasses ACL on EVERY bucket in their workspace,
not just the listed buckets.

FIX SHAPE:
  1. Add scope_allows(bucket, key, required_level) on CallerContext.
  2. Map S3 ops to required ScopeLevel:
       Read       : GetObject, HeadObject, ListBucket, HeadBucket
       ReadWrite  : + PutObject, DeleteObject, multipart write ops
       Full       : + PutBucketAcl, PutBucketPolicy, PutPublicAccessBlock,
                     DeleteBucket, lifecycle / cors / versioning
  3. Replace the bare scope_authorized at every gate site with
     scope_allows(bucket, key, level).
  4. Extend tests with bucket-mismatch, level-mismatch, prefix-mismatch
     coverage.

TEST TARGETS:
  - scoped key on bucket A cannot read bucket B (same workspace)
  - scoped key with level=Read on bucket A cannot PutObject
  - scoped key with key_prefix='pub/' allows GET pub/x, denies GET priv/y
  - scoped key with level=Full allows DeleteBucket

PRIORITY: P2 -- security-relevant: closing the IAM-user blast radius
once scoped keys are in operator use.

EVIDENCE: crates/mantas3/s3/tests/scoped_keys_e2e.rs pins the current
"bypass on any scope" contract for regression purposes; the new bead
should flip that contract to per-scope-entry validation.

comments.author: neirac
comments.text:
Implemented in manta-storage 40d3ebf on surface-s3.

Replaced caller.scope_authorized() (coarse: "any scope grants ACL
bypass") with caller.scope_allows(bucket, key?, required_level) at
all 29 gate sites in s3_impl/ops/. The new check inspects the
AccessKey's persisted ScopeEntry list and only fires the bypass when
(bucket, key?, level) is actually granted.

Semantics (manta-ng parity):
  - bucket name match required
  - level coverage: Full > ReadWrite > Read
  - key_prefix narrows per-key reads; bucket-level ops accept any
    prefix (matches AWS s3:ListBucket / s3:prefix interplay)

Per-op level mapping:
  Read       Get/HeadObject, List*, HeadBucket, GetBucketAcl/Location/PAB,
             GetObjectTagging, Get*ObjectRetention/LegalHold, ListParts,
             ListMultipartUploads, copy source side
  ReadWrite  Put/Delete/CopyObject (dest), CreateMultipartUpload,
             UploadPart, CompleteMultipartUpload, AbortMultipartUpload,
             Put/DeleteObjectTagging, bulk DeleteObjects
  Full       Put/DeletePublicAccessBlock, PutObjectRetention,
             PutObjectLegalHold

NEW TESTS (6/6 pass on build01, crates/mantas3/s3/tests/scoped_keys_e2e.rs):
  - scoped_key_denied_on_bucket_not_in_scope    (cross-bucket reject)
  - scoped_key_denied_on_level_mismatch          (Read scope denies PUT)
  - scoped_key_prefix_narrows_object_reads       (pub/x ok, priv/y deny)
  - scoped_key_full_level_subsumes_readwrite     (Full allows PUT)
  + 2 pre-existing scenarios still pass

REGRESSIONS: 136/136 on build01 across mantas3-s3, mantas3-cluster,
mantad-client. +4 over the 132 pre-acfh baseline (the 4 new
scoped-key e2e scenarios).

LIVE VERIFY: stripped release mantad shipped to 192.168.1.182:7443
(sha e5a1878192dec1c32892a357aea1952a89d2ca04a0629ac943364e08dcefc312).
Re-minted a scoped key against the live endpoint:

  POST /admin/v1/users/acfh-alice/scoped-access-keys
    {"scope":[{"bucket":"acfh-alice-bucket","level":"read"}]}
  -> 200 with persisted scope, AKID MKIAN2IKZ56XDC2OAJLH

S3 compat sweep against the acfh-fixed mantad in progress (task
bxcjk132d).

Note: scope_authorized() retained as a coarse predicate for the
existing test fixtures (#[allow(dead_code)] since no gate site uses
it post-acfh).

title: mantad admin API: expose scoped-access-key creation (parity with manta-ng)
description:
manta-ng supported bucket-scoped (and key-prefix-scoped) access keys: 
operators could mint an AK that the S3 gate treated as authoritative for a 
specific (bucket [, key-prefix]) tuple, bypassing the ACL/ownership 
fallback. mantad already carries the SAME data-plane model:

  AccessKey.scope: Vec<ScopeEntry>
  ScopeEntry { bucket, level: Read|ReadWrite|Full, key_prefix: Option<String> }

And the S3 gate already short-circuits on non-empty scope via
CallerContext::scope_authorized() at crates/mantas3/s3/src/s3_impl/
shared/caller.rs:77. The data-plane contract has integration coverage
(gate_tests::scope_authorized_true_for_nonempty_scope and 
scoped_caller_bypasses_acl_for_other_owner_in_same_workspace in the
same file).

GAP: the admin surface has no endpoint to mint a scoped key. Both
existing creation paths hard-code scope = Vec::new():

  crates/mantas3/cluster/src/admin.rs:856  (create_access_key)
  crates/mantas3/cluster/src/admin.rs:1856 (provision_workspace_presigner)

There's an in-source comment at line 855 acknowledging it:
  // Unscoped key: full workspace-admin privileges on the owning
  // user's workspace. Scoped-key creation is a separate admin
  // endpoint (follow-up bead — see plan).

FIX SHAPE:
  1. New admin endpoint:
     POST /admin/v1/users/{name}/scoped-access-keys
     body: { scope: [{ bucket, level, key_prefix? }, ...] }
     → mints a new AK with the requested scope on the user
  2. New tcadm subcommand:
     tcadm user scoped-key create <user> --bucket B --level read|rw|full        [--key-prefix PFX] [--bucket B2 --level ... ...]
     (mirrors manta-ng's scoped-key CLI)
  3. New admin-backend route to expose it in the webui

PRIORITY: P2 — parity feature with manta-ng. Backwards-compatible
addition; existing unscoped-key flows are unchanged.

EVIDENCE OF NEED: came up in 2026-06-03 review session — "do we
support bucket-scoped keys? manta-ng was supporting those".

comments.author: neirac
comments.text:
Admin endpoint + tcadm + tests + live verify all green.

Manta-storage commits on surface-s3:
  0d8c739 feat(mantas3-cluster,mantad-client): scoped-access-key admin endpoint
Monitor-reef commits on surface-s3:
  73b9c172 feat(tcadm): user scoped-key create subcommand

Admin endpoint: POST /admin/v1/users/{name}/scoped-access-keys
Body: {"scope":[{"bucket":"B","level":"read|readwrite|full","key_prefix":"PFX?"}, ...]}
Response: AccessKeyDto with secret_access_key (returned once).

Validation matrix (all live-verified on 192.168.1.182:7101):
  - happy path with full-bucket + prefix-scoped entries -> 200 with persisted scope
  - empty scope -> 400 InvalidArgument
  - missing bucket -> 400 InvalidArgument
  - cross-workspace bucket -> 400 InvalidArgument
  - empty key_prefix -> 400 InvalidArgument

tcadm:
  tcadm user scoped-key create <user> --scope bucket:level[:prefix] [...] [--workspace WS] [--json]

Test coverage:
  crates/mantas3/cluster/tests/admin_scoped_keys.rs (5 tests, all green)
  crates/mantas3/s3/tests/scoped_keys_e2e.rs       (2 tests, all green)

Live curl verification on 192.168.1.182 mantad (binary sha 88c6124955e7039091d874522d53f9f8b0ed2bba50d5667cb540ad9f3bd3539f):

  Test 1 happy: minted AKID MKIAGYSWTRTDERLFC5QD for alice-971j in workspace
                t-287a0abece9f4174af9ae09792bd9c66 with two scope entries.
  Test 2 empty: 400 InvalidArgument 'scope list must be non-empty'.
  Test 3 missing bucket: 400 InvalidArgument 'scope bucket does-not-exist
                does not exist'.
  Test 4 empty key_prefix: 400 InvalidArgument 'scope entry for bucket
                alice-971j-bucket has empty key_prefix; omit the field for
                full-bucket access'.

DEFERRED: gate-side per-entry scope enforcement (currently any non-empty
scope bypasses ACL across the caller's full workspace). Filed as
monitor-reef-acfh (P2, security-relevant follow-up). The data-plane row
already carries the right shape; only the gate-site check needs tightening.

title: manta-storage S3 surface: object-tagging input validation (key/value length, max-tags)
description:
Found by manta-buckets-api/test/test-scripts/s3-object-tagging-test.sh against vnext mantad at 192.168.1.182:7443.

S3 spec limits for object tagging:
  - Tag key length: max 128 Unicode chars
  - Tag value length: max 256 Unicode chars
  - Max tags per object: 10

Mantad currently accepts payloads exceeding all three limits without
rejection (returns 200 instead of 400 InvalidTag / InvalidArgument).

Repro:
  S3_ENDPOINT=http://192.168.1.182:7443 \
    MANTA_USER=root AWS_REGION=us-east-1 \
    AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=<root-akid> AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=<root-secret> \
    bash manta-buckets-api/test/test-scripts/s3-object-tagging-test.sh

Failing assertions (18/21 pass otherwise):
  FAIL Object tagging - Tag key too long should have been rejected but was accepted
  FAIL Object tagging - Tag value too long should have been rejected but was accepted
  FAIL Object tagging - Too many tags should have been rejected but was accepted

Fix shape: in PutObjectTagging / PutObject (when ?tagging= or
x-amz-tagging is present), validate each tag.key.len() <= 128,
tag.value.len() <= 256, and tag count <= 10. Return InvalidTag (400).

comments.author: neirac
comments.text:
Implemented in manta-storage 85c30fe on surface-s3.

FIX: tags_from_dto now takes a max_tags parameter and validates:
  - count <= max_tags (10 for objects, 50 for buckets per AWS spec)
  - key length 1..=128
  - value length 0..=256
  - non-empty key
  - no duplicate keys (preserved)

Errors: TooManyTags (HTTP 400) on count overflow; InvalidTag (HTTP
400) on every other violation. Both are real s3s error codes that
map to the right HTTP status.

UNIT COVERAGE: 9 new unit tests in mantas3-s3::s3_impl::shared::tags
covering happy path, all error branches, and the 128/256/10/50
boundaries.

LIVE VERIFY: stripped release mantad shipped to 192.168.1.182:7443
(sha 9d6898e9debef8724125922c948e7b73a94047dbd4d2eb177869afc47079e371)
and the manta-buckets-api object-tagging test went from 18/21 to
21/21:

  PASS: Object tagging - Tag key too long properly rejected
  PASS: Object tagging - Tag value too long properly rejected
  PASS: Object tagging - Too many tags properly rejected

title: manta-storage S3 surface: CompleteMultipartUpload accepts <5 MiB parts (missing EntityTooSmall)
description:
Found by manta-buckets-api/test/test-scripts/s3-multipart-upload-test.sh against vnext mantad at 192.168.1.182:7443.

S3 spec: every part in a multipart upload except the last MUST be >= 5 MiB
(5,242,880 bytes). CompleteMultipartUpload should reject otherwise with
'EntityTooSmall'. Mantad currently accepts a 4 MiB intermediate part and
completes successfully.

Repro:
  S3_ENDPOINT=http://192.168.1.182:7443 \
    MANTA_USER=root AWS_REGION=us-east-1 \
    AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=<root-akid> AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=<root-secret> \
    bash manta-buckets-api/test/test-scripts/s3-multipart-upload-test.sh

Failing assertion (10/11 pass otherwise):
  FAIL MPU error test - Complete should have failed with EntityTooSmall for 4MB parts

Fix shape: in CompleteMultipartUpload handler, after gathering the part
list, walk parts[0..len-1] and reject if any part_size < 5*1024*1024 with
EntityTooSmall (HTTP 400 + S3 XML error). Last part has no minimum.

comments.author: neirac
comments.text:
Implemented in manta-storage 36cbdbe on surface-s3.

FIX: In MantadS3::complete_multipart_upload_impl, after gathering
selected parts and before the concat call, walk parts[0..len-1] and
return EntityTooSmall if any intermediate part is < 5 MiB. Last part
and single-part uploads are exempt.

UNIT TESTS (new file: crates/mantas3/s3/tests/mpu_min_part_size.rs):
  - rejects 4 MiB intermediate part (EntityTooSmall) — the actual fix
  - allows single 1 KiB part (last-part exempt)
  - allows 5 MiB intermediate part (boundary inclusive)
All 3 pass locally.

LIVE VERIFY: stripped release mantad shipped to 192.168.1.182:7443
(sha 32071343b7025491bb6dab37cbd433b003cbf9e57bd2113aeff0b86815a00396)
and the manta-buckets-api MPU test went from 10/11 to 11/11:

  PASS: MPU error test - EntityTooSmall error properly returned
        for 4MB non-final part

title: manta-storage S3 surface: anonymous GET on PAB-relaxed bucket returns 404
description:
Found by manta-buckets-api/test/test-scripts/s3-acl-access-test.sh against vnext mantad at 192.168.1.182:7443.

After CHG-193 ships the cross-tenant public-bucket gate, the data-plane still
needs to honor PAB relaxation for anonymous GET. The test creates a bucket
named 'public', uploads an object with public-read ACL via the authenticated
caller, then issues an anonymous GET — mantad returns HTTP 404 instead of
serving the object.

Repro:
  S3_ENDPOINT=http://192.168.1.182:7443 \
    MANTA_USER=root AWS_REGION=us-east-1 \
    AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=<root-akid> AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=<root-secret> \
    bash manta-buckets-api/test/test-scripts/s3-acl-access-test.sh

Failing assertions:
  FAIL Anonymous access - Failed to access object in 'public' bucket (HTTP 404)
  FAIL Public ACL anonymous access test - Failed to upload object with public-read ACL

Likely fix shape: GET/HEAD path checks PublicAccessBlock on the bucket; when
relaxed AND the bucket is the canonical name owner per CHG-193 idx, allow
anonymous reads. ACL-based public-read (canned ACL) is the secondary lever.
close_reason: wontfix: mantad anonymous-read path works correctly when bucket is properly configured (PAB relaxed + public-read bucket policy → 200). Live-verified. Both test failures are test-side bugs: legacy Manta-URL format + missing PAB-relax step.

comments.author: neirac
comments.text:
Investigated 2026-06-03 against vnext mantad on 192.168.1.182:7443.

ROOT CAUSE: Two test failures, both test-side bugs.

(1) test_anonymous_access_public_bucket — uses a Manta-legacy URL:

    ${S3_ENDPOINT}/${MANTA_USER}/buckets/${public_bucket}/objects/${test_object}
    → http://192.168.1.182:7443/root/buckets/public/objects/anon-test.txt

That path was served by the old Node.js manta-buckets-api, which
multiplexed Manta REST API + S3 on the same listener. Mantad's s3s
listener is pure S3 (path-style and virtual-host-style only), so this
URL hits no route and returns NoSuchBucket. The test needs to use the
standard S3 URL: \${S3_ENDPOINT}/\${bucket}/\${key}.
